title: Headie One: In Their Own Words
author: Allastair Voss
image_alt: Headie One press photo by Frank Fieber
body:
[spotify:artist/6UCQYrcJ6wab6gnQ89OJFh]

Headie One released EDNA in October 2020 to a Metacritic score of 87, placing it among the most critically acclaimed UK drill records ever made. Born Irving Ampofo Adjei and raised on Broadwater Farm in Tottenham, he built a body of work that treats geography as argument: the estate is not backdrop but source material, the ground from which everything grows. With The Last One arriving in 2024, that decade-long narrative found the close it had been building toward.

**ArtOnly: UK drill has reached a genuinely global audience. What is it about the form that makes it travel so far?**

"Drill is very honest. When you're not sugar-coating anything and you're telling the whole truth, you're just being true to yourself. It's why drill is everywhere now, because it's real. I think everyone can relate in some way and that's why it will always grow." (Clash Magazine, 2022)

Honesty as a mechanism for universality is not a new argument, but Headie One grounds it in craft rather than posture. His flows carry the compressed syntax of North London block life, yet listeners from Seoul to Sao Paulo have cited his records as reference points. When he says everyone can relate, he means the weight of constrained circumstance rather than the specifics of any one postcode. Drill travels because it documents the conditions that limitation creates, and limitation is not local.

**ArtOnly: EDNA is named after a community centre on Broadwater Farm. How does a place become the architecture of a record?**

"Edna is a story that could only come from Tottenham because of all of the things that I've seen and experienced that I poured into this." (The FADER, February 2021)

"That's the mud that you come from, it made me who I am, both positive and negative." (The FADER, February 2021)

Naming an album after a community centre is quietly radical. It insists the institution matters, that the social fabric of an estate deserves the same weight as the postcode mythology that surrounds it. The guest list on EDNA, running from Stormzy and Burna Boy to Drake and Skepta, was assembled with attention to tone rather than commercial calculation. Every feature either extends the album's emotional logic or sharpens its argument. The record does not escape Broadwater Farm. It maps it, in detail, for anyone willing to look.

**ArtOnly: You have spoken honestly about how close you came to not being here at all. How do you carry that now?**

"I really could have been dead before my first album, let alone my second." (Wonderland Magazine, July 2024)

"It got to the point where I wasn't really good at anything else so I had no choice really but to keep it going, even though I was doing a lot of other bad stuff as well. It was the only thing away from the bad stuff I was doing. I feel like the music saved me." (Wonderland Magazine, July 2024)

The candour here is not performance. Headie One served a custodial sentence in 2019, was recalled on licence while his profile was growing, and has spoken publicly about the friction between what he was building and the restrictions placed on him. That tension does not appear in the music as wound. It appears as pressure, converting into precision. The records are as controlled as the life that produced them was not, and that contrast is part of the argument.

**ArtOnly: You once described something close to revenge as a central motivating force. What does that look like when you are working?**

"That's the driving force, that's the main thing that's pushing me to do well. It's this feeling inside of almost like revenge because of how bad it was before, and that's been key." (The FADER, February 2021)

It is worth drawing a precise distinction. Revenge here does not mean settling personal scores. It means proof: a demonstration aimed not at enemies but at the structures that assumed the worst. The chip he carries is systemic. Opportunity withheld, access restricted, potential met with surveillance and suspicion. His records exist in a space those structures cannot reach, and they accumulate into evidence that the assumption was wrong every time it was made.

[spotify:album/066XCkY2VUxaJHf5g3tHJx]

**ArtOnly: The Last One closed a chapter you felt had been left unfinished. What did it feel like to finally reach that point?**

"When I was making this album, I realised I was at the point I wanted to be when I dreamed about making music to actually doing it." (NME, June 2024)

"With this album, it's showing the end of the beginning and enjoying the space and mindset I'm in now." (NME, June 2024)

The phrase end of the beginning suggests a shift in register rather than arrival. Headie One is not describing completion so much as a change in the quality of motion, the point at which a life spent reacting can pivot into one spent choosing. That shift is audible across The Last One. Productions reach toward space rather than compression. His cadences carry more air. He sounds, for the first time, like someone who can afford to take stock of what he has already built and decide what to do with the foundation.

**ArtOnly: When everything else is stripped back, what is rap at its core for you?**

"To rap is to tell your story. You got rappers from Essex might not have the same identical experience of council block living in West London." (NME, June 2024)

This is Headie One's most compact artistic statement. Rap as autobiography requires specificity, and specificity demands that a rapper understand precisely where they come from. The Essex reference is not a dismissal. It is a reminder that the form only functions when the map is accurate and the mapmaker knows what they are drawing from. Distance from the source degrades the signal, and the signal is everything.

Headie One has moved from Broadwater Farm to Wembley Arena without erasing the route between them. His records function as testimony: granular, geographically anchored, built from accumulated experience rather than projected aspiration. The Last One did not announce an ending. It established a new baseline, the ground level from which whatever comes next will be measured. What follows arrives from a position already earned, by someone who survived long enough to choose what to do with it.
